title: Unsatisfactory Radiographs: Causes and Cures
narrationScript: Sooner or later you will hang a film on the illuminator and know immediately that something went wrong. The density is off, the image is flat, the edges are fuzzy, or there are marks on the film that have nothing to do with the weld. A Level two is expected to do more than notice. You are expected to name the cause and state the cure, because every retake costs money, dose, and schedule. The good news is that almost every bad radiograph traces back to one of three places. The exposure, the processing, or the way the film was handled and stored. Learn to ask which bin the symptom belongs in, and troubleshooting becomes a routine instead of a mystery. Start with density, because it is the first thing you measure. If the film is too dark, it received too much exposure, or the processing amplified a normal one. Common causes are an exposure figured from a stale decay chart, a source to film distance shorter than the chart assumed, kilovoltage set too high, or a developer that is too warm, too concentrated, or running too long. Remember how sensitive exposure is to distance. Cut the distance from thirty six inches to twenty four and the film receives more than twice the exposure. If the film is too light, turn the same list around. The classic field cause is forgetting that a gamma source decays. An iridium one ninety two source loses about half its strength every seventy four days, and exposure times must grow to match. Cold, exhausted, or under replenished developer produces the same thin look. Before blaming the technique, check the processing. A density problem that shows up on every film from one shift, regardless of part or technique, is almost always the darkroom. Now contrast. Contrast that is too high sounds like a gift, but it means the film cannot hold the whole part. The thin sections go black, the thick sections stay clear, and only a narrow band in between is readable. The causes are kilovoltage too low for the thickness range, or a film and technique combination with more gradient than the job needs. The cures are to raise the kilovoltage, which widens the latitude, or to load two films of different speed in the same cassette so one film covers the thin range and the other covers the thick. Contrast that is too low is the more dangerous failure, because a flat film can look acceptable while hiding flaws. Causes include kilovoltage too high, scatter reaching the film because nobody masked or collimated, density so low that you are reading the toe of the characteristic curve, underdevelopment, and fog of any kind. The cures follow directly. Bring the energy down, control the scatter, expose to a healthy density between about two and three and a half, and develop fully at the correct time and temperature. Definition is the third leg. A fuzzy image comes from geometry, contact, or motion. The geometric part you can calculate. Unsharpness equals the source size, times the object to film distance, divided by the source to object distance. A big source, a short shot, or a film standing off from the part all blur the edges. Poor contact between the film and its screens blurs the image too, and so does any movement of the source, the part, or the film during the exposure. Sharp films come from small sources, long distances, film tight against the part, and rigid setups. Then there is fog, the gray veil that lowers contrast everywhere. Fog has four classic sources. Light, from a leaking darkroom or an unsafe safelight. Radiation, when film is stored too close to sources or scatter. Chemistry, from contaminated or oxidized developer or from fumes. And age or heat, when film sits too long or too warm before use. Test your safelights honestly. Give a film a slight uniform exposure first, because film carrying a latent image is far more sensitive to fogging. Cover part of it on the bench, leave it out for your normal handling time, develop it, and look for a boundary. If you can see where the cover sat, the safelight is fogging your film. Light leaks behave differently from general fog. They print localized dark areas. A leaking cassette signs film after film with the same dark edge band, and a leaking darkroom marks films near where they sit during loading. Stand inside the darkroom, let your eyes adapt for several minutes, and inspect the door frame, the pass box, and every penetration. Finally, artifacts, the false images. Crimp the film before exposure and you get light crescent shaped marks. Crimp it after exposure and the crescents are dark. Static discharge draws dark branching lines that look like lightning on dry winter days. Bubbles clinging to the film in the developer leave light round spots. Dirt between the screen and the film prints sharp light specks, and a scratched screen signs every film from that cassette in the same place. The professional habit is simple. Identify every mark before you interpret the film, and never call a discontinuity until you have ruled out an artifact.
imagePrompt: A radiographic film interpreter at a darkened viewing station tilts a long weld radiograph in front of a glowing high-intensity illuminator, examining a crescent-shaped mark on the film surface under the beam of a small desk lamp.

sections.heading: Three Bins: Exposure, Processing, or Handling
sections.body:
Almost every unsatisfactory radiograph traces to one of three places: the **exposure** (technique, source, geometry), the **processing** (chemistry, time, temperature, agitation), or the **handling and storage** of the film before and after the shot. A Level II sorts the symptom into a bin before touching anything:

- **Scope.** Does the problem appear on every film from a shift, regardless of part or technique? Suspect processing or a common darkroom fault. Tied to one technique or part? Suspect the exposure. Tied to one cassette or one loader? Suspect handling or equipment.
- **Surface check.** Tilt the film in reflected light. A mark on the emulsion surface - scratch, dirt, residue - is an artifact, not an image.
- **Recurrence check.** A mark repeating at the same spot on films from the same cassette points at the screens or cassette, not the part.

Then work the film in order - density first, then contrast, then definition, then artifacts - because each problem can masquerade as the next, and density errors corrupt every judgment made after them.

sections.heading: Density Too High, Density Too Low
sections.body:
**Film too dark.** The film received too much exposure, or development amplified a normal one. Field causes in rough order of frequency: exposure time from a stale decay chart or wrong activity; source-to-film distance shorter than the chart assumes - by the inverse square law, moving from 36 in. to 24 in. raises film exposure by (36/24)² ≈ 2.25×; kilovoltage above the chart value; developer too warm, too concentrated, or timed long. Cures: recompute with today's activity and the actual SFD, and verify developer time and temperature against the time-temperature chart.

**Film too light.** Mirror the list. The classic gamma cause is uncorrected **source decay**: Ir-192 halves about every 74 days, so a chart exposure computed six weeks ago delivers only about two-thirds of the intended film exposure. Add SFD longer than planned, part thicker than assumed, kilovoltage low, cold developer (below about 65 °F), exhausted or under-replenished chemistry, or development cut short. Cure: correct the technique with the decay curve and true thickness, and restore processing to standard **before** re-shooting - otherwise you are tuning two variables at once.

If every film from one shift runs thin or dark regardless of job, check the darkroom before blaming any technique.

sections.heading: Contrast Too High, Contrast Too Low
sections.body:
**Too much contrast** means the film cannot hold the part's thickness range: thin sections go black, thick sections stay clear, and only a narrow band between them is readable. Causes: radiation energy (kV) too low for the **thickness range**, or a film/technique combination with more gradient than the job needs. Cures: raise the kilovoltage, which widens latitude, or load **two films of different speeds** in one cassette so the faster film reads the thick metal and the slower film reads the thin - the double-film technique.

**Too little contrast** is the more dangerous failure, because a flat film can look acceptable while hiding flaws. The causes stack: kilovoltage too high; **scatter** reaching the film (no masking, no collimation, backscatter from floor or walls - watch the lead letter B check); density so low you are reading the **toe** of the characteristic curve, where the gradient collapses; underdevelopment; fog of any origin; and a wide-latitude film class used where a high-contrast class was needed. The cures answer one-for-one: use the lowest kV that penetrates, control scatter with masks, collimators, filters, and backing lead, expose into the 2.0-3.5 density range where the curve is steep, develop fully to time and temperature, and store film so it stays fog-free.

sections.heading: Poor Definition
sections.body:
A fuzzy image has three families of cause - **geometry, contact, motion**:

- **Geometric unsharpness**: Ug = F·t/d, where F is the source or focal-spot size, t the object-to-film distance, and d the source-to-object distance. Example: a 4 mm source with t = 25 mm and d = 500 mm gives Ug = 4 × 25 / 500 = **0.2 mm**. A bigger source, a shorter shot, or film standing off the part all widen the penumbra, and codes cap Ug as a function of part thickness.
- **Poor film-screen contact**: any gap between the lead screens and the film lets the electrons they emit spread before landing. A warped cassette, a sprung rigid holder, or a loose vacuum pack blurs the image locally.
- **Motion**: movement of source, part, or film during exposure smears the image directionally - IQI wires sharp in one direction and blurred in the other are the tell. Clamp the rigging, keep guide tubes and cranks still, and shield the setup from wind and traffic.

Graininess adds its own softness: coarse, fast film classes and pushed development both raise visible grain. Use the slowest film class the exposure economics allow.

sections.caption: The three causes of poor definition: geometric unsharpness from source size and distances, loss of film-screen contact, and motion during exposure.

sections.heading: Fog and Light Leaks
sections.body:
**Fog** is a uniform veil of developed silver that raises base density and crushes contrast everywhere on the film. Four classic sources:

- **Light** - a leaking darkroom, or an unsafe safelight: bulb too strong, wrong filter, lamp too close, film out too long.
- **Radiation** - film stored near sources, bays, or scatter paths fogs silently before it is ever loaded.
- **Chemical** - contaminated or heavily oxidized developer; chemical fumes reaching stored film.
- **Age and heat** - outdated film, or film stored warm; expect elevated base-plus-fog and flat images.

Test safelights honestly: give a film a slight uniform exposure first (film carrying a latent image is far more sensitive to fogging than raw film), cover strips of it for graded times under normal working conditions, develop, and look for boundaries between the strips.

**Light leaks** print **localized** dark areas rather than a uniform veil. A leaking cassette signs film after film with the same dark edge band; a leaking darkroom marks films irregularly near where they sit during loading. To find darkroom leaks, stand inside with the lights out, dark-adapt for five to ten minutes, and inspect the door frame, pass box, and every penetration.

sections.title: Rule out the artifact before you call the flaw
sections.body: Before reporting any indication, prove it is in the part: tilt the film in reflected light to check for surface damage, check whether the mark repeats on other films from the same cassette or hanger, and where two films were loaded together, compare them - a true discontinuity image appears on **both** films in the same place; an artifact almost never does.

sections.heading: Artifacts: The Field Catalog
sections.body:
Learn these signatures cold:

- **Crimp (crescent) marks** - sharp kinks in the sheet. Kinked **before** exposure: light crescents. Kinked **after** exposure: dark crescents.
- **Static marks** - dark branching, tree- or lightning-shaped lines (sometimes clusters of dots) from sliding film fast in dry air. Move slowly, ground yourself, keep darkroom humidity up.
- **Pressure marks** - local density change wherever film was pinched, rolled over, or stepped on.
- **Scratches** - check with reflected light; abrasion before development often reads dark, gouges after processing read light.
- **Air bells** - light round spots where bubbles clung during development; tap hangers on immersion and agitate.
- **Chemical splashes** - developer droplets before processing print dark spots; fixer droplets print light or clear spots.
- **Streaks and surge marks** - uneven density trailing from hanger clips or film edges, from missing or wrong agitation, exhausted developer, or weak stop action.
- **Water and drying marks** - rings and runs from uneven drying; use wetting agent and a clean final rinse.
- **Screen and cassette marks** - dirt or lint between screen and film prints sharp light specks; a scratched screen repeats its mark on every film from that cassette.

Every one of these can mimic - or hide - a real discontinuity.

sections.caption: Simulated film strips showing classic artifact signatures: crimp crescents, static discharge, air bells, a cassette light-leak band, screen-dirt specks, and agitation streaks.

figures.title: Why definition goes soft: geometry, contact, motion

interactive.title: Cause and Cure: Sort the Symptom
interactive.intro: Read each complaint from the reading room and file it where the fix lives: the exposure technique, the processing room, or film handling and storage.

items.label: Density 3.9 across the image - the crew set up at 24 in. SFD using a chart written for 36 in.
items.why: Inverse square law: (36/24)² ≈ 2.25 times the intended exposure reached the film. Recompute for the real SFD.

items.label: Films keep running thin on chart values - the Ir-192 decay curve was last consulted six weeks ago
items.why: At a 74-day half-life, six weeks costs roughly a third of the activity; exposure times must grow as the source decays.

items.label: Weld image flat and washed out after the operator raised the kV far above the chart to shorten the shot
items.why: Excess energy slashes subject contrast. Use the lowest kV that penetrates the section in reasonable time.

items.label: Every film from Monday morning reads about 0.7 low in density; the developer thermometer shows 60 °F
items.why: Cold developer underdevelops every film regardless of technique - the classic processing-bin clue is a whole shift affected.

items.label: Round light spots on the image; bubbles were seen clinging to the film as the rack entered the developer
items.why: Air bells block developer locally and leave light spots. Tap the hanger on immersion and agitate properly.

items.label: Streaky density trailing from the hanger clips on films left motionless in the tank
items.why: Surge and streak marks come from missing or improper agitation and tired chemistry.

items.label: Dark branching, lightning-like lines on films pulled fast from the cassette in 15 % RH winter air
items.why: Static discharge exposes the emulsion. Handle film slowly, ground yourself, and raise darkroom humidity.

items.label: Light crescent-shaped marks where a sheet was kinked while being pushed into a rigid holder
items.why: Crimp damage before exposure prints light crescents; the same kink after exposure would print dark.

items.label: A gray veil over films from a fresh box that spent a month on a shelf beside the source storage bay
items.why: Radiation fog from poor film storage - unexposed film must live far from sources and scatter paths.

items.label: The same dark band along one edge of every film that comes out of cassette number seven
items.why: A recurring edge band tied to one cassette is a light leak in that cassette - repair or scrap it.

checkQuestions.question: A technique chart calls for a 10-minute exposure at 20 in. SFD. Access forces the crew to shoot at 40 in. instead. To hold the same film density, the new exposure time is about:
checkQuestions.explanation: Doubling the distance cuts intensity at the film to one quarter (inverse square law), so exposure time must increase by (40/20)² = 4: from 10 minutes to about 40.

checkQuestions.question: Crescent-shaped marks on a processed film appear lighter than the surrounding image. When was the film most likely crimped?
checkQuestions.explanation: Kinking before exposure desensitizes the emulsion locally and prints light crescents; kinking after exposure marks the latent image and prints dark crescents.

checkQuestions.question: Which group of causes would ALL push radiographic contrast down?
options: Kilovoltage too high, uncontrolled scatter, underdevelopment, density on the toe of the curve
Kilovoltage too low, tight collimation, full development, density of 2.5
Slow film class, long SFD, fresh developer, good masking
Small focal spot, short development, low fog, density of 3.0
checkQuestions.explanation: Excess energy, scatter, underdevelopment, and reading on the low-gradient toe of the characteristic curve each flatten the image - together they produce the dangerous low-contrast film.

checkQuestions.question: A shot uses a 0.2 in. source, an object-to-film distance of 1.5 in., and a source-to-object distance of 30 in. The geometric unsharpness is:
checkQuestions.explanation: Ug = F·t/d = 0.2 × 1.5 / 30 = 0.01 in. Increasing the source-to-object distance or reducing the offset shrinks the penumbra further.

checkQuestions.question: Why is a film with a slight prior uniform exposure preferred for testing safelights?
options: It develops faster, so the test takes less time
A film carrying a latent image is much more sensitive to additional fogging exposure, so the test reflects the worst case
It is cheaper than using fresh film
checkQuestions.explanation: Real films under the safelight are partially exposed, and a latent image makes the emulsion several times more responsive to low-level light - testing raw film would understate the fogging hazard.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Film Quality and Manufacturing Processes Course §2.7 (unsatisfactory radiographs - causes and cures)
Industrial radiographic film manufacturers' processing and troubleshooting guidance (generic reference)
ASME Section V-style density, backscatter check, and geometric unsharpness requirements (generic reference)
